basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Naṣr — “Divine Help”, also called sūrat al-tawdīʿ (سُورَة التَّوْدِيع), “the sūra of farewell” — is among the last sūras revealed, near the end of the Prophet's mission. But the Sufi reading raises it to a still higher pitch. Qushayrī writes of the help and the opening: “The help of God to him was that He annihilated him from his own self (afnāhu ʿan nafsihi, أَفْنَاهُ عَنْ نَفْسِه), He removed from him the rulings of humanity (aḥkām al-bashariyya), and purified him from the troubles of the soul. As for the opening, it was that He raised him to the place of proximity (maḥall al-dunuww), made him His chosen one through the qualities of nearness, clothed him in the garment of union (libās al-jamʿ), drew him to Himself out of himself, and unveiled for him the secrets of the Truth that had been hidden before.” The sūra recounts, beyond the historical victory at Mecca, the inward victory: that in which the servant, stripped of his own self, is given back to God — and the outward mission accomplishes itself in the total arrival in God. Tustarī had said it differently: “Help your spirit (rūḥ) against your lower self (nafs) — for the spirit comes from the Hereafter and longs for it; the soul comes from the world and falls back into it.”

versets.trad: When God's help and victory come,
versets.commentaire: al-fatḥ (الفَتْح), “the opening”, names the conquest of Mecca in year 8 of the Hijra — not by arms, but by this rare Arabic word for conquest: to open. But Qushayrī carries the verse further: “the help of God” in its secret meaning is the effacement (fanāʾ) of the servant in Him; and “the opening” is the lifting to the place of nearness, and the unveiling (kashf) of the secrets that were hidden. A vertiginous reading: the victory the sūra names is not only one people over another, but the soul drawn out of itself. Naṣr, the help, becomes the withdrawal by which God lifts the veils; fatḥ, the opening, becomes the irruption of the Face where there had been only the self.

mots.def: temporal particle: when, at the moment that.
jāʾa: to come, to arrive, to befall.
naṣr: help, assistance making victory possible; in the Sufi reading, the effacement of the self by God Himself.
Allāh: God; here in the genitive.
coordinating conjunction: and.
fatḥ: opening, breakthrough; here the conquest of Mecca (the “opening”), but also, in its spiritual sense, the irruption of Truth into the heart.

versets.ar: وَرَأَيْتَ النَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ أَفْوَاجًا
versets.tr: wa-raʾayta n-nāsa yadkhulūna fī dīni Llāhi afwājā
versets.trad: and you see mankind entering the Religion of God in throngs,
versets.commentaire: Historically, afwājan (أَفْوَاجًا) — in throngs, in successive bands — describes the massive arrival of tribes after the opening of Mecca. In the Sufi reading, Tustarī applies the verse to the person: “Help your spirit (rūḥ, رُوح) against your lower self (nafs, نَفْس), by preparing for the Hereafter — for the spirit comes from there and longs for it. The soul desires the world because it comes from it; the spirit desires the Hereafter because it comes from it. Gain ascendancy over the soul, open for it the door of the Hereafter by glorification (tasbīḥ) and asking for forgiveness.” The servant too has his opening to receive, his entrance to witness: the moment when his own faculties, one by one, come in throngs to bow before the Presence.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
raʾā: to see; here in the perfect with a present sense in the conditional.
an-nās: mankind, the people, humanity.
dakhala: to enter; here in the imperfect plural: they enter.
preposition: in, into.
dīn: religion, way, due order.
Allāh: God.
fawj: a troop, a band; in the plural afwāj, “in successive waves”.

versets.ar: فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَاسْتَغْفِرْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُ كَانَ تَوَّابًا
versets.tr: fa-sabbiḥ bi-ḥamdi rabbika wa-staghfirh, innahu kāna tawwābā
versets.trad: hymn the praises of your Lord and ask forgiveness of Him. He, truly, welcomes every repentance.
versets.commentaire: At the moment of success, the command is not to rejoice, but to glorify (sabbiḥ, سَبِّحْ) and to ask forgiveness (staghfir, اسْتَغْفِرْ). Tustarī: “He accepts the repentance, again and again, each time the servant returns to Him.” Tawwāb (تَوَّاب) is among the tenderest of Names — the One-who-keeps-turning-back. And the Arabic word tawba names both man's return to God and God's return to man — the same root for the two movements. According to a saying of Ibn ʿAbbās, on hearing this sūra Abū Bakr wept: “O Messenger of God, your death has been announced to you.” And the Prophet said to him: “You have spoken the truth.” The historical opening of Mecca is also, in secret, the announcement of the Prophet's final Return to his Lord — and this is why the sūra ends, without lingering, on praise, forgiveness, and the great word tawwāb.

mots.def: linking particle: then, so; introduces the consequence.
sabbaḥa: to declare God free of all imperfection, to proclaim His transcendence; imperative.
ḥamd: praise.
rabb + suffix pronoun.
conjunction: and.
istaghfara: to ask forgiveness; ghafara means to veil — to ask that God cast a veil over what stands between Him and us.
mots.gram: imperative + suffix pronoun, 2nd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: emphasis particle inna + suffix pronoun.

mots.sens: He is, He has always been
mots.def: kāna: to be; in the perfect, names a lasting state.
tawwāb: intensive form; a divine Name — the One who welcomes repentance and turns back to the servant. The same word, tawba, names also man's repentance: two movements for one reality.
